pattern: A pair sums to a multiple of k exactly when its two remainders mod k add up to k (or are both zero), so counting how many elements fall into each remainder bucket turns pairing into a bucket-matching problem instead of a search over combinations.
hints: Only the remainder of each number modulo k matters for whether a pair sums to a multiple of k -- two remainders r and (k - r) always pair off cleanly.

Group every number by its remainder mod k (using a mod that's always non-negative, since numbers can be negative). Elements with remainder 0 must pair only with each other, so there must be an even number of them.

For every other remainder r, the count of elements with remainder r must exactly equal the count of elements with remainder (k - r) -- including the special case r == k - r (when k is even), which again needs an even count within that single bucket.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, smaller array that pairs up
negative multiples of k pairing with positive ones
zeros and values that are exact multiples of k
k of one, every pairing trivially works
even k where the halfway remainder bucket must itself be even
large-magnitude values near the constraint bounds

stress.description: large random even-length arrays with varied k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size if size % 2 == 0 else size + 1
    n = max(2, n)
    k = rng.randint(1, 100000)
    arr = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [arr, k]
